title: App on the Wrong Port
scenario: A web application is deployed and claimed to be 'running', but curl localhost:8080 returns Connection refused. A junior engineer insists the app is healthy. Your investigation reveals the app IS running — just not on the port everyone expects. This is a classic misconfiguration where the app's startup config doesn't match the documented port.
symptoms: The application process IS running (visible in ps aux)
ss -tulnp shows a listener — but NOT on 8080
mission: Use ss -tulnp to discover which port the application is actually listening on. Record the actual port to /home/intern/actual_port.txt (just the number), then confirm the application works by running curl localhost:ACTUAL_PORT.

hints.title: Confirm the failure
hints.content: Run 'curl localhost:8080'. You'll get Connection refused. Before assuming the app is down, check whether it's listening somewhere else.

hints.title: List all listeners
hints.content: Run 'ss -tulnp'. Look at every entry in the Local Address:Port column. Is there a port other than 8080 showing a python3 process?

hints.title: Record the actual port
hints.content: Once you find the real port number, run: echo "8081" > /home/intern/actual_port.txt

hints.title: Verify the app
hints.content: Run 'curl localhost:8081' — you should get a valid HTTP response (directory listing). The app was never broken, just misconfigured on the wrong port.
